retrieved_docs.title: Selenium Deficiency -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium is an essential trace mineral that is vital in maintaining human health. Selenium primarily exists as selenoproteins, which have diverse roles in the body's regular physiological processes and metabolism. [1] In particular, glutathione peroxidase (G-Px), a selenoprotein, contributes to antioxidant defense, safeguarding the body against the damaging effects of reactive oxygen and nitrogen species. Another significant selenium-dependent function involves iodothyronine deiodinases, which facilitate the conversion of inactive thyroxine or tetraiodothyronine (T4) into the active thyroid hormone triiodothyronine (T3). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Cellular Level -- Post-translational modification
retrieved_docs.content: Once in the ER, the pre-pro-polypeptide undergoes post-translational processing where three major modifications are made to the pre-pro-polypeptide for it to become pro-collagen. The signal peptide on the N-terminal is removed The lysine and proline residues get additional hydroxyl groups added to them via hydroxylase enzymes which require vitamin C as a cofactor Glycosylation of the selected hydroxyl groups on lysine with galactose and glucose b Three of the hydroxylated and glycosylated pro-a-chains assemble by twisting into a triple helix by zipper-like folding. The triple helix configuration is 3 left-handed helices twisted into a right-handed coil Now the pro-collagen molecule is ready to move to the Golgi apparatus for final modifications and assembled into secretory vesicles to enter the extracellular space

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Function -- Selenium
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium functions as an essential component of selenoproteins that play major roles in dense against oxidation, thyroid hormone formation, DNA synthesis, reproduction, and fertility. The functions of most selenoproteins are unknown, but the known functions involve participation in antioxidant and anabolic processes. A family of antioxidant enzymes named glutathione peroxidases is dependent upon selenium to function to neutralize hydrogen peroxide and organic hyperoxides in both intracellular and extracellular compartments. Deiodinases are a group of three selenoenzymes responsible for converting T4 to T3 in thyroid hormone activation. Selenoprotein-P is the most abundant selenoprotein found in plasma and plays a major role in the transport and homeostasis of selenium in tissues. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Essential Amino Acids -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Besides 20 amino acids that participate in protein synthesis, recently, 2 more new amino acids have been described—selenocysteine and pyrrolysine. Selenocysteine occurs at the active site of several enzymes, including thioredoxin reductase and glutathione peroxidase. Pyrrolysine is not present in humans but is used in the biosynthesis of proteins in some methanogenic species, such as archaea and bacteria. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Selenium -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium exerts its biological functions via molecules called selenoproteins, containing the amino acid selenocysteine. The human genome encodes 25 selenoproteins. [13] Selenium plays a role in forming several biologically significant molecules/molecular families in humans and other mammals. Some of these include: Glutathione peroxidases - this family of enzymes catalyzes the reduction of organic hyperoxides and hydrogen peroxide. [14] Thioredoxin reductase - This enzyme functions to form reduced thioredoxin, which serves as a source of electrons for enzymes that play a role in making DNA from RNA precursors. [15] Deiodinase enzymes - These enzymes activate and deactivate various thyroid hormones and hormonal metabolites. There has been speculation that selenium supplementation can help prevent Hashimoto thyroiditis by reducing thyroid peroxidase antibodies, but data remains equivocal. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Selenium Deficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium is an essential trace mineral that is vital in maintaining human health. Selenium is predominantly found in the form of selenoproteins, which play a crucial role in the body's physiological processes and metabolism. This activity explores the multifaceted roles of selenium, facilitated by selenoproteins, encompassing antioxidant protection by glutathione peroxidase against oxidative stress, selenium-dependent thyroid hormone conversion, immune system modulation, and its impact on HIV progression.

retrieved_docs.title: Selenium Deficiency -- Pathophysiology -- Endocrine System
retrieved_docs.content: Among the 35 identified selenoproteins, 3 hold a significant role and are recognized as iodothyronine deiodinases—pivotal contributors in thyroid hormone metabolism. Notably, the thyroid gland contains the highest selenium concentration among all organs in the human body. The initial iodothyronine deiodinase converts inactive T4 into its active form, T3. The second enzyme is abundant within the central nervous system, brown fat, and skeletal muscles, contributing to the activation of thyroid hormones. The third enzyme is involved in deactivating thyroid hormones, serving as a crucial regulatory element in thyroid function. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Selenium -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The requirement of selenium in the endocrine system is defined by its role in making active thyroid hormone. The mineral interacts with iodothyronine deiodinase, an enzyme that transforms inactive thyroid hormone (T4) into active thyroid hormone (T3). [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Hashimoto Thyroiditis -- Etiology -- Selenium status
retrieved_docs.content: Selenoproteins are essential for thyroid function and protection. [20] [21] The important thyroidal selenoproteins are the iodothyronine deiodinases, glutathione peroxidases, and thioredoxin reductases. These enzymes are involved in thyroid hormone metabolism, maintenance of cellular metabolism and regulation of redox state, and protection from oxidative damage from reactive oxygen species and hydrogen peroxide. [22] [23] Deficiency in this mineral has been associated with a higher risk of Hashimoto thyroiditis. A systematic review and meta-analysis from 2024 showed that thyroid antibody levels were reduced with selenium supplementation, independent of underlying selenium deficiency and thyroid hormone replacement. Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) levels also reduce with selenium supplementation, independent of thyroid hormone replacement. The certainty of this evidence was moderate. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Fibril Assembly
retrieved_docs.content: Lysyl oxidase a copper-dependent enzyme acts on lysine and hydroxylysines, and covalent bonding between tropocollagen molecules form a collagen fibril

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Essential Amino Acids -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Approximately 20,000 unique protein-encoding genes are responsible for more than 100,000 unique proteins in the human body. Although hundreds of amino acids are found in nature, only about 20 are necessary to synthesize all the proteins in the human body and most other life forms. These 20 amino acids are all L-isomer, α-amino acids. All amino acids, except for glycine, contain a chiral αcarbon. All these amino acids are L-isomers with an R-absolute configuration except for glycine and cysteine. Glycine lacks a chiral center, whereas cysteine has an S-absolute configuration due to its sulfur-containing R-group. Selenocysteine and pyrrolysine are considered the 21st and 22nd amino acids, respectively. More recently discovered amino acids may be incorporated into protein chains during ribosomal protein synthesis. Although pyrrolysine has biological functionality, humans do not incorporate it into protein synthesis. When translated, these 22 amino acids may also be modified through a post-translational modification to add further diversity in generating proteins. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Keshan Disease -- Pathophysiology -- Selenium's Role as an Antioxidant
retrieved_docs.content: Dietary selenium is a necessary micronutrient for both humans and mammals. [19] Selenium directly integrates into polypeptide chains as seleno-L-cysteine (SeCys), contributing to 25 identified SeCys-containing proteins known as selenoproteins in humans. [20] Notably, glutathione peroxidases (GPxs) use selenoproteins to catalyze the reduction of peroxide species (R-OOH) into alcohols (R-OH). Selenium is crucial in fortifying the body's antioxidant defense against the oxidative damage induced by respiration. [21] Individuals with Keshan disease exhibit lower expression of glutathione peroxidases in their hearts compared to healthy subjects due to selenium deficiency. [22] This deficiency contributes to the onset of myocardial disease. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Subclinical Hypothyroidism -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Subclinical hypothyroidism characteristically presents with normal thyroxine (T4) levels and elevated thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) levels. The incidence of subclinical hypothyroidism is estimated at 3% to 15%, depending on the population studied. Minor fluctuations in T4 levels result in considerably larger fluctuations in TSH levels. Although TSH levels exhibit wide variability across the population, intra-individual variation remains minimal, which is secondary to a unique individual setpoint within the hypothalamic-pituitary axis for each person. This condition correlates with an increased risk of fatal and non-fatal coronary artery disease events, congestive heart failure, and fatal stroke. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of subclinical hypothyroidism, emphasizing the collaborative role of the interprofessional healthcare team in enhancing patient care. This activity also helps clinicians gain insights into distinguishing characteristics within relevant literature to maximize patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Hypothyroidism -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The thyroid hormone plays an essential role in energy metabolism, growth, and neurodevelopment. Specifically, the thyroid hormone acts on neuronal differentiation, synapsis development, and myelination in the prenatal and newborn periods, regulating central nervous system development. The thyroid hormones are derived from the amino acid tyrosine and produced by the thyroid gland in response to stimulation by the thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) produced by the anterior pituitary. TSH is regulated by the thyrotropin-releasing hormone (TRH), which is released from the hypothalamus. This regulation pathway is called the hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid (HPT) axis. There are two active thyroid hormones, thyroxine (T4) and triiodothyronine (T3).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2175
retrieved_docs.content: Triiodothyronine (trijodthyronin) is a thyroid hormone that is synthesized and secreted by the thyroid gland (štítná žláza). The štítná žláza is also the primary anatomical site where thyroid insufficiency (قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة), a condition characterized by reduced production of thyroid hormones, is identified. Recognizing the relationship between trijodthyronin and the štítná žláza, as well as the gland’s role in قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة, is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective management of disorders affecting thyroid hormone metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2568

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 639
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement therapy is a treatment approach that utilizes the substance selenium, with the selenium supplement serving as the active component of this therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1253
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement is classified as a member of sodium selenate, indicating that the supplement’s active ingredient is the compound sodium selenate, which provides the essential trace element selenium. Recognizing that selenium supplement belongs to the sodium selenate group helps clinicians understand its chemical composition, potential bioavailability, and safety considerations when using it for nutritional support or therapeutic purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1520
retrieved_docs.content: mixture of endocrine hormones, neurotransmitters, minerals, vitamins, amino acids, and herbal constituents in oral liquid formulations, reflecting a therapeutic strategy aimed at modulating the hypothalamic‑pituitary‑adrenal‑thyroid axes, supporting sympathetic activity, and addressing broad metabolic and stress‑response needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1443
retrieved_docs.content: Antithyroid preparations are a class of medications that inhibit thyroid hormone synthesis, and they encompass specific agents such as diiodotyrosine and carbimazole. Both diiodotyrosine and carbimazole function as antithyroid drugs, sharing the common therapeutic purpose of reducing excessive thyroid hormone production in conditions like hyperthyroidism. Recognizing that these compounds belong to the broader category of antithyroid preparations helps clinicians select appropriate treatment options and understand their pharmacologic relationships.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 437
retrieved_docs.content: The thyroid gland (štítná žláza) is the organ that synthesizes thyroid hormones (hormony štítné žlázy) and specifically produces triiodothyronine (trijodthyronin). When the function of the thyroid gland is impaired, it can result in hypothyroidism (قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة), a condition marked by insufficient hormone production. Recognizing the relationship between the thyroid gland, its hormones, and hypothyroidism is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1783
retrieved_docs.content: Selenium supplement therapy utilizes the substance selenium, referred to as “selen,” as its active component. The Selenium supplement itself is directly linked to selen, indicating that the supplement’s therapeutic effect derives from this element. Selen is classified as a mineral, belonging to the broader category of minerály. Consequently, the Selenium supplement functions as a mineral‑based therapeutic agent, with its efficacy rooted in the use of selenium within the supplement formulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2472
retrieved_docs.content: The thyroid gland (štítná žláza) is the organ that produces the thyroid hormones (hormony štítné žlázy), specifically thyroxin and trijodthyronin. These hormones are synthesized and secreted by the gland and play a central role in regulating metabolism. The thyroid gland is also the anatomical site where hypothyroidism (قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة) is identified, reflecting a deficiency of its hormonal output. Understanding the relationship between the gland, its hormones, and the condition of hypothyroidism is essential for accurate diagnosis and effective management of thyroid‑related disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2618
retrieved_docs.content: The štítná žláza (thyroid gland) produces the hormones thyroxin and trijodthyronin, linking these two substances directly to thyroid function. Both thyroxin and trijodthyronin are essential thyroid hormones that regulate metabolism, growth, and development throughout the body, underscoring the central role of the štítná žláza in endocrine health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroid Extracts and Thyroid Inhibitors comprise a category that includes the subclass thyreostatika, which are agents specifically related to the function of the štítná žláza (thyroid gland). Both thyreostatika and the thyroid hormone trijodthyronin (triiodothyronine) are directly associated with the štítná žláza, reflecting their roles in modulating thyroid activity. The štítná žláza is also identified as the anatomical finding site for قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة (hypothyroidism), underscoring the clinical relevance of these substances and the gland in the diagnosis and management of thyroid disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1105
retrieved_docs.content: Thyroxin and trijodthyronin are hormones that have a direct relationship with the štítná žláza (thyroid gland). The štítná žláza is the anatomical site where the condition قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة (hypothyroidism) is identified, reflecting a deficiency in the production or activity of these thyroid hormones. Recognizing how thyroxin and trijodthyronin are linked to the štítná žláza is essential for the accurate diagnosis and effective management of قصور الدَّرَقِيَّة.
